speaker: interviewee
text: How they say? The wives usually say my husband thinks he died and he's in heaven when-- when they retire in Florida. It was-- it's a good life here after all those many, many years of hard work. When I started working I was working seven days a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: That sounds [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we came penniless.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is March 27, 1995. Survivor is Lewis Fagen. Interviewer, Cathy Epstein, in Boca Raton, Florida, United States of America. Language is English. This is tape number one. Today's date is January 27, 1995. My name is Cathy Epstein, C-A-T-H-Y E-P-S-T-E-I-N. I'm interviewing Lewis Fagen, L-E-W-I-S F-A-G-E-N, in Boca Raton, Florida, United States of America. The language is English. Could you please tell me your full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lewis Fagen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that your name at--

speaker: interviewee
text: My middle name is L-- Ludwig, which was my name in Poland. My previous name was Ludwig Faigenbaum. They called me Ludek.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: November 28, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which makes you how old today?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- what city and country were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Krakow, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your family? What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Jacob. My mother's name was Natalie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a sister, Janine, who perished during the war, practically at the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your father make a living in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, as a young boy, at the age 16, was apprenticed to a goldsmith, where he learned the trade. Then the First World War came, and he served in the Austrian Army. And right after the war, he was still working as a goldsmith until the age of 23. At the age of 23, his brother, who was working for a firm dealing in bicycles, told him that this is a great business in Poland at the time. And my father quit goldsmithing and went into bicycle business. He opened a store with bicycles, music instruments, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] phonographs, records, sporting goods. Eventually it developed into three stores in Krakow until the 1930s, when the depression hit Europe. And he got into financial difficulties, and eventually, at the end of the '30s, he had to give it up. And he became a distributor of the biggest Polish factory of bicycles, distributor for all-- all of Poland of Kaminski bicycles. And he also participated in manufacturing the bicycle parts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your family life growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] at the time, my family was quite well to do. And I had a sister, who was a terrific young woman. A pianist. Very musical. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- I-- we went to school, which was one of the finest schools in-- in-- in all of Poland, I think, which was the Hebrew Gymnasium of Krakow, where we had tremendous comradeship among the students. And in fact, we developed such friendships that until today, whoever survived, we are still in touch and still meet. Wherever we are in this world, we meet every 5 or 10 years in Israel. Every summer, we used to go to the mountains on vacations. And this-- this were always memorable vacations. My father and I used to hike the moments in summer. I used to go to winter camps where I was skiing. I started skiing at the age of seven, and I loved that sport. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother was working in my father's business, but we had a housekeeper who was taking care of us. All in all, even though it was Poland, the life was good for us. We have-- we have good memories of childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: As far as anti-Semitism is concerned, we heard something. For instance, I had a cousin who-- who was studying at a law school in Krakow. And I know-- he was a much older cousin-- that he was walking to the university with-- with a cane in his hand. I asked him, why do you walk with a cane? He says, well, I need the cane to defend myself when I'm attacked by-- by other students because I'm Jewish. So all in all, it was good. I belonged to an organization of Jewish students, which was sort of-- they called it korporatsiya, which was a fraternity like-- actually was a-- a fraternity that was Zionistic, very Zionistically minded. But the language of the fraternity was German, because it was based on German fraternities. Krakow was very Germanized sort of a city, because it was under influence of Austria always. It was part of Austria for many, many years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father, for instance, was very fond of times when Austria was occupying Krakow, when he served in the Austrian Army, where he always thought of the emperor of Austria, Franz Joseph, as being a friend of the Jews. So we were sort of Germanized in Krakow. Most of us did speak German, besides taking German language in school. In school, we were taking up Hebrew subjects, Latin, and German. We-- other elected-- elected languages was Greek, Classical Greek, or even-- or French. The school was the center of our life. Everything turned around the school and my schoolmates. I was [? never ?] a student because I was always busy with other things, like soccer, like cycling, like skiing, like running. I was a runner in school. I played in the school band, clarinet. This was the-- this was the center. And that school, until to today, we writing about it, we talking about it, and we can't forget it. That's-- that's about the kind of life we had. We lived in a fairly nice apartment. It was sort of on the outskirts of the Jewish section of Krakow. We had a company car in which my father used to come every weekend, wherever we were on our summer vacations. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All in all, it was a happy life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your Jewish upbringing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the Jewish upbringing I was getting in school. The school was not a religious school. However, we had religious subjects. We had Hebrew literature. We had-- we studied Gemara and Talmud in Hebrew. We had Jewish history, Jewish religion classes. But on the whole, I would say that student in this particular school were not from very religious homes. The majority was not. There were some that were. For instance, my father belonged to a temple that was called in Krakow a progressive temple. Progressive temple meant that we had a great choir. And in the choir, we had gentiles singing. Women didn't sit together with men. We wore head covering. However, most of the people who belonged to the temple were sort of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] not exactly temple-going people. The temple Friday night or Saturday morning was practically deserted, which was odd for a city like Krakow, which was a very Jewish city with a great Jewish traditions. And my father kept his stores open on Saturday, which was also very unusual in those circumstances. In-- in our home, my parents didn't speak Yiddish. I didn't know any Yiddish until I came to United States, where I learned Yiddish, only because my mother didn't speak Yiddish. However, my grandfather was a very pious Hasidic Jew, my father's father. And my father was the first rebel in the family. He rebelled against the strict orthodoxy, and he just turned around. So-- however, I must say that I went home was extremely Zionistic. My father was always Zionist. We always talked about Palestine. My father's dream was to go one day to Palestine with the whole family. And-- and our school was Zionistic. And we-- we-- we remain Zionistic until today. I hope that something rubbed off of on my children, and I think it did. So as far as Jewish life, religiously, I wouldn't say very pious. But nationalistically, yes, we were-- we were very Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: You celebrated-- do you remember celebrating holidays at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the holidays. I mean, the seders were unforgettable. The whole family came together. We always had somebody for a Friday night dinner-- outsiders, or-- or poor relatives, or whoever. And Friday night was-- my father came home from-- from his business and ate dinner. And after dinner, he went back to the business. But he always had dinner with us. My father was [NON-ENGLISH] every morning, but he never went on Saturday to temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was sort of an odd way of-- I think his [NON-ENGLISH] was sort of superstitious. He wouldn't leave home unless he did it. So the Jewish life was-- was very much there. And, you know, if you live in a country that is anti-Semitic, and you're being persecuted from time to time, you become more Jewish. Do you remember any incidences after Hitler's rise to power in 1933 that life changed or anything started changing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. The Polish government, until 1935, there was a dictator by name of Pilsudski, who was-- Joseph Pilsudski was a marshal in the Polish Army. And he was sort of protective of the Jewish people. And we felt fairly secured while he was alive. When he died in 1935, we started feeling pressures-- governmental pressures on Jews. They were coming out with all kind of laws as far as kashrut was concerned. I mean, they were mostly laws-- anti-Jewish laws. And we felt that-- that this government may do something drastic as far as Jews were concerned. We always were very unsure of ourselves. There was a marshal, Rydz-Smigly, and there was a prime minister who was very anti-Semitic. So on the level of the government and on our standing in Poland, we didn't feel secure since 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned your family was very Zionistic. Was there any talk of leaving Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the conversations?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time my father was talking about it, but never was the proper time. We talked about it at the-- the dinner table and with friends-- I remember when people used to come to the house. But there was never a proper-- not yet. In another year. Another. Let the children get education first. Let's do this. Let's do that. He-- he-- he did want to leave for Palestine. Yes, he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you notice that things started changing for you in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as what? As far as--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- rules being made, or your life changing. When the war broke out, did you notice--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the war out, there was tremendous change. Well, I was-- the school was closed in December of 1939. The Germans walked in September 5, 1939 in the city of Krakow. When the Germans walked in, we were standing on the streets watching the German Army marching in. We never expected-- well, we knew of Nazi anti-Jewish laws, but we never expected of the extreme happenings, what would happen. And if we would have known it, by the way, we-- we-- we would have probably left. My father, a few days before the war started, Kaminski owned a big factory of bicycles, which factory was in Warsaw, sent a truck for us to bring us to Warsaw, because we felt that Krakow will be occupied in no time, because Krakow was close to the German and Austrian borders. However, Warsaw will hold off. So Kaminski sent a truck for us. We were all packed, sitting on the valises, waiting for the truck to come and take us to Warsaw. The truck never arrived, because then we found out that the truck was requisitioned by the Polish Army coming on their way to Krakow. So-- and it-- luckily so, because if we would have wound up in Warsaw, we would have wound up in Warsaw Ghetto. And very few peopled survived that-- that ghetto. My father-- men started leaving as the German Army was getting closer. And he left my mother, and my sister, and myself, and he left for the east. And the men marched east all the way practically to the Russian border. And my father spent some time in city of Lvov, which was an eastern Polish city, under the Russian occupation there. He-- we were alone, and we had no means to-- to live on, because all the savings accounts in the bank were-- were closed by the Germans, so we couldn't touch our money. And the money wasn't worth that much either. So my mother was selling some of her jewelry in order to sustain us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 18. And so when the Germans walked in, the first months, nothing was happening. But then in October already, there was a van that was owned by a German cheese company. And we called the van Kaesewagen. When the van showed itself up in the Jewish section of Krakow, we knew that they're catching Jews for hard labor. And sure enough, as soon as the van drove into the Jewish section, few German soldiers ran out of it and were catching people on the street who were close to the van, and putting them in the van, taking them for hard labor. Some came back. Some didn't. This I remember-- that-- that van I remember vividly. It even had a picture a-- of cheese on it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The real searches-- I think the first time that they sealed off the Jewish section of Krakow was around December of 1939. One very early morning, 5 or 6 o'clock, we heard goosestepping. And we looked out the window, and we saw the Germans [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] marching into the section. And every, let's say, half a block, two soldiers standing. And we knew that something is up, but we didn't have the experience yet. And sure enough, then we found out that they're going from home to-- house to house, searching for valuables, for-- looking through the different papers. And so we were sitting the house, afraid of what's going to be next. And sure enough, a German officer with three soldiers walked into the house and asked, bring out all the valuables. But luckily, my mother had the foresight-- what she did before, because we expected something like that, we had very heavy doors in the apartment. The doors were about, I would say, probably 2 inches thick. And we knew a carpenter, whom we call. And he made a cavity at the bottom of the doors with a strip of wood that was on two screws. The doors were taken off the hinges. In that cavity, my mother was hiding all the jewelry with the two strips. So whenever we needed a piece of jewelry, we had to take the door off the hinges, take the strip of wood, and take a piece of jewelry, and put the door back on the hinges.

speaker: interviewer
text: So most of the valuables were hidden there, so they hardly found anything. But it was a frightful experience, the way they behave, the way they-- they turned everything around in the house, the way they were taking out drawers and throwing everything on the floor. It was the first terrible experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another experience that I had personally is when I walked on the street of Krakow, I was stopped by a Ukrainian collaborator who asked me whether I'm Jewish. And I had to show him my papers. And I said, sure enough, I was Jewish. He-- they took me to the fire department, and they put me on a truck. And we were going through Krakow, hanging Nazi flags for the arrival of Governor General Frank was arriving at that time in Krakow. And they kept me for about eight, nine hours. My mother didn't know what was happening to me. And finally, one of the firemen, who knew my father, I asked him, please let me go, because my mother will die-- die of fright. And he-- he let me go back. Around January, my father returned. 1940, my father returned from Russian side. Back-- came back to the family. He was caught on the border of Russia and Germany. However, the officer in charge was Austrian. My father showed him his pictures in an Austrian Army uniform, and he let him-- let him out. And he came back to Krakow. We were forced to work. I mean, everybody had to be employed someplace. My first employment was in the-- I loved the outdoors. So I got myself employed in the gardens of Krakow, fixing beds, and planting things, and so on. In wintertime, we were forced to shovel snow throughout the city. All the Jewish men had to shovel snow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these jobs that you were paid for?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Then my father had a very different office, who was a importer of Italian accordions. And I-- I used to play a little bit the accordion. So-- and the firm was taken over by a Ukrainian-- what they call [SPEAKING GERMAN]. [SPEAKING GERMAN] in German is man of confidence for the German-- German government. They put somebody like this in confidence to-- to-- to do conduct business and take it out of the Jewish hands. And this man was put in, this Ukrainian, who was a very sort of sympathetic man, quite intelligent. He used to be a-- a editor of a Ukrainian newspaper in eastern Poland. And he had a son my age who served in the Sonderdienst, which was a special unit of the German Army. And he got to like me, and we-- He took me around Krakow. And I was taking off my armband while walked with him. And I worked for them--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me about when you were working at the accordion--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was actually a packing and shipping clerk at that place. And that work I enjoyed, because whenever I had free time, I could tinker around on the accordion. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That finally ended when we were required to have, second half of 1940-- by the end of 1940, they were giving out what they called Kennkarte. Kennkarte was an identification that whoever had identification could remain in city of Krakow. By January of 1941, they started building the ghetto on the outskirts of Krakow, in-- in the outskirts called Podgorze, which was on the other side of the Vistula River. And whoever had a Kennkarte could remain in Krakow. Whoever did not get a Kennkarte had to leave Krakow, and they didn't care where.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we did not receive the Kennkarte. And my father made arrangements with a gentile man who-- who owned a house in the outskirts, in a town called [NON-ENGLISH]. He had a little house there. And my father paid him good money, so he-- he gave us a room and the usage of a kitchen. And we had to leave our apartment and move to that room.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I lost my job. I couldn't go to my job at the accordion place. However, I secured another position, because I had to work. In the ghetto itself was a private company repairing automobiles for the Germans. And I was very anxious to learn this automobile repair. I felt that maybe if I learn it, I'll be needed to work. So I secured a job there, and I got a permit to travel 10 miles from the suburb where we stayed to the ghetto. And I traveled every day on a bicycle to work-- to my work in the automobile works. We stayed at the suburbs, actually, for about, I would say, 10, 11 months. My father didn't do anything. He actually was-- there were some other friends that moved to the same suburb, and playing cards and doing nothing. My sister secured a little spinet piano someplace, and she was practicing for days long. And I was traveling to work every day to the ghetto. One day, they said they're going to liquidate. Everybody from the suburbs has to move to the ghetto. They finally established the ghetto for 25,000 people. And we all moved to the ghetto. When we moved to the ghetto, we were assigned one room with two windows. Each-- for-- for each window, they assigned three people. So when the room had two windows, six people had to live in that room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were the four of us, plus my father's sister's daughter, my cousin, and another friend of my cousin. We-- all six of us lived in that one room. And another family lived there, a very orthodox family, people who were manufacturing brushes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was our experience. I started-- I was still working at the automobile works for a while. And then one day, they closed up the automobile works and I had to find another position. And there was a big repair shop called [NON-ENGLISH] for German Army vehicles, where I also got a job repairing motorcycles. My father, in the meantime-- the-- an Austrian by the name of Julius Madritsch, who was a garment manufacturer in-- in-- in Vienna, established big factory manufacturing uniforms little outside the ghetto. And people from ghetto were going there. My-- my father was one-- one of the first to-- to-- to get work there. And he was going out to Madritsch, and he became quite friendly with Julius Madritsch, the owner of the factory who was an Austrian gentile. My mother and my sister were going out to another garment factory called Optima, which was at one time Optima Chocolate Factory. The Germans converted into garment factory, where the women were also manufacturing uniforms.
